Event: Chang abuses and bullies his Spanish students using his authority in class
Explanation: He indulges his power-trip at their expense, gaining gratification while seriously harming their learning and comfort.

Event: Chang teaches Klingon instead of Spanish, despite being hired as a Spanish teacher
Explanation: He sabotages his own job security and credibility while depriving students of the subject they need.

Event: Chang fakes being a qualified Spanish teacher and works with a phony degree
Explanation: He gains a job and status in the short term while misleading the school and students academically.

Event: Annie records Chang admitting he has no teaching/Spanish credentials and exposes him to the Dean
Explanation: She risks some interpersonal fallout to stop an unqualified, abusive teacher and improve the school situation.

Event: Greendale keeps Chang employed because he is the only Spanish teacher available
Explanation: They preserve short-term staffing at the cost of educational quality, liability, and student welfare.

Event: Chang secretly serves as a mole for City College at Greendale
Explanation: He seeks revenge and leverage through infiltration while putting the school at serious risk.

Event: Chang creates and plays the “Kevin with Changnesia” persona to infiltrate Greendale for City College
Explanation: He regains access and some sympathy while undermining trust and safety on campus.

Event: Jeff surveils Chang instead of openly confronting him once he suspects Changnesia is fake
Explanation: He protects the group by quietly monitoring a dangerous person while avoiding an unprovable public accusation.

Event: Chang kidnaps the Dean and nearly blows up the school with explosives
Explanation: His terror plot endangers everyone including himself with almost no lasting benefit and heavy backlash.

Event: Chang almost burns down the whole school trying to kill the study group with fireworks
Explanation: His attempt at murder would have destroyed the school and likely killed him too for no stable gain.

Event: Chang uses a squad of youth to seize control of Greendale and install a fake dean figurehead
Explanation: He gains power and control but destabilizes the institution and abuses minors in the process.

Event: Chang enforces ludicrous policies as de facto commander of Greendale
Explanation: His rule feeds his ego and control but damages campus life and operations.

Event: Chang repeatedly betrays the study group whenever they show trust in him
Explanation: He extracts short-term advantage or attention while eroding relationships that could benefit him long-term.

Event: Chang switches sides multiple times during “A Fistful of Paintballs”
Explanation: He maximizes his tactical fun and advantage during the game while causing confusion and minor harm to others.

Event: Chang nearly commits a deadly act of terrorism at Greendale and later gets released on work release
Explanation: They reduce his punishment to reclaim his labor despite the extreme risk he poses to the same community.

Event: Chang accepts a math professor job at Greendale despite his past attempt to blow up the school
Explanation: He gains status and income, but his presence endangers credibility and safety for everyone else.

Event: Greendale hires Chang as a math teacher again after his prior crimes
Explanation: They fill a staffing hole by re-employing a known dangerous, unstable person.

Event: The study group and Dean initially believe in “Kevin” and Changnesia and give Chang a second chance
Explanation: They emotionally invest in and empower him, which risks their own safety but offers him a path to reform.

Event: The group later files a lawsuit against the Dean for rehiring Chang as a teacher
Explanation: They protect themselves and future students, accepting conflict with the Dean to correct a dangerous decision.

Event: Chang Fakes Amnesia throughout Season 4 to escape punishment for his Season 3 crimes
Explanation: He avoids serious legal consequences while maintaining proximity to those he’s harmed.

Event: Chang eventually owns up to his crimes, serves a brief jail sentence, and goes on work release
Explanation: He accepts some punishment, gains limited freedom, and partially restores legitimacy in others’ eyes.

Event: Chang sits with ostracized Jeff at lunch and helps him reconcile with the group
Explanation: He forgoes the advantage of Jeff’s pariah status to improve Jeff’s situation and group cohesion.

Event: Chang goes easy on Jeff for trying to expose his fake Changnesia
Explanation: He spares Jeff retaliatory harm despite the threat Jeff poses to his scheme, preserving potential goodwill.

Event: Chang lies to Alessandra that he doesn’t remember her despite wanting reconciliation
Explanation: He clings to his cover at the cost of their relationship and her emotional well-being.

Event: Jeff reintroduces Chang to Alessandra, forcing Chang to choose between truth and his cover
Explanation: He risks awkwardness to test Chang’s honesty and potentially restore a marriage or expose the lie.

Event: Chang chooses to keep pretending not to remember Alessandra, missing a chance at reconciliation
Explanation: He sacrifices a relationship he still values for a fragile deception that cannot last.

Event: Chang punches anyone who draws a swastika, even by accident
Explanation: He upholds a personal standard and vents aggression while inflicting physical harm regardless of intent.

Event: Chang makes racist comments toward Shirley, Troy, Annie, and Abed despite hating racism toward himself
Explanation: He gains momentary superiority or laughs while directly harming others with hostility.

Event: Chang invents and uses the term “Changnesia” to brand his fake condition
Explanation: The gag serves his con and ego while misleading others about his mental state.

Event: Jeff accidentally starts using Chang’s “Smurfing” wordplay after mocking it
Explanation: He mildly undermines his own stance and dignity while giving others amusement.

Event: Chang drags Annie out of class, assigns a punitive essay, and extends its length for every protest
Explanation: He asserts dominance and vindictive control at heavy cost to students’ workload and morale.

Event: Chang makes the punitive essay worth 30% of the grade and schedules a “big-ass quiz” immediately after
Explanation: He strengthens his grading power and fear factor while unfairly jeopardizing students’ academic standing.

Event: The school tries for years to fire Chang but fails because no one wants his job
Explanation: They recognize the harm but keep a toxic teacher, prioritizing staffing convenience over quality.

Event: Annie exposes Chang’s credential fraud to the Dean, leading to his firing
Explanation: She risks retaliation to remove an abuser and fraud, greatly benefiting future students.

Event: Chang threatens to deep-fry a dog, eat a mom’s face, and wear a brother’s skin as pajamas during a children’s play
Explanation: He heightens his performance and personal thrill while terrifying and disturbing kids and parents.

Event: Chang becomes a drunk-with-power head of campus security, evolving into a wannabe warlord
Explanation: He gains authority and status while abusing it to the detriment of campus safety and climate.

Event: Chang organizes and leads an army of twelve-year-old boys to enforce his rule
Explanation: He gains an obedient force by exploiting minors and militarizing the campus.

Event: Chang threatens the families of kids watching “Celebrity Pharmacology” while playing a drug personification
Explanation: He pushes his performance and ego while deeply unsettling his young audience.

Event: Chang abandons his “Kevin” persona and reverts to villainy in the Season 4 finale
Explanation: He chooses short-term power/chaos over the long-term benefits of reform and acceptance.

Event: Chang’s betrayal in the Season 5 finale includes stealing the million dollars entrusted to him
Explanation: He secures a huge financial gain while betraying trust and harming institutional finances.

Event: The school board entrusts Chang with a million dollars despite his history
Explanation: They expose major funds to a proven unreliable figure for negligible safeguarding benefit.

Event: Chang betrays the Save Greendale Committee again in Season 5 after being accepted by them
Explanation: He trades sustained belonging for another short-lived advantage and attention spike.

Event: Abed recognizes Chang as the person who originally handed out flyers that brought the group to Greendale
Explanation: He reframes Chang’s role positively, which boosts Chang’s inclusion and group understanding.

Event: Abed invites Chang for fro-yo and tells him he’s been part of the group all along
Explanation: He offers Chang belonging and goodwill with minimal cost to himself.

Event: Chang aggressively tries to join the study group throughout Season 2 despite their resistance
Explanation: His pushiness undermines his own acceptance while occasionally giving them comic diversion.

Event: The study group finally accepts Chang into the group and committee in Season 5
Explanation: They gain an extra member and some of his positive contributions, though with risk, while he gains belonging.

Event: Chang betrayingly sides against the group so often that “Chang betrays us” ends up on their to-do list
Explanation: They normalize anticipating betrayal, which helps planning but admits ongoing self-exposure to harm.

Event: Chang pulls off a powerful lead performance in a stage production of The Karate Kid
Explanation: He earns respect and self-worth through genuine talent, delighting others.

Event: Chang delivers a rousing speech at Garrett’s wedding that helps save the day
Explanation: He gains social credit and emotional fulfillment by improving a crisis moment for everyone.

Event: Chang calmly and helpfully supports Shirley during childbirth
Explanation: He behaves admirably, easing her experience and slightly redeeming himself.

Event: Chang saves Jeff from being a complete pariah by helping him reconcile with the group
Explanation: He intentionally sacrifices leverage over Jeff to restore group harmony.

Event: Chang goes along with being monitored by Jeff after Changnesia suspicion without immediately retaliating
Explanation: He avoids confrontation that could expose him, while allowing Jeff to feel proactive, minimizing escalation.

Event: Chang becomes campus security after Season 2 and gradually builds power from that role
Explanation: He gets stable authority and purpose but uses it in ways that later harm the campus.

Event: Chang joins the main group as a “convenient replacement” after Troy leaves and takes Troy’s seat
Explanation: He gains the closeness he craves while the group fills a functional and narrative gap.

Event: The study group includes a Chang figurine peering in from outside in their meta diorama
Explanation: They acknowledge his constant presence in a playful way, slightly validating him and deepening group self-awareness.

Event: Chang uses insulting racist nicknames for his students
Explanation: He feeds his ego and sense of control at direct emotional cost to them.

Event: Chang coins and performs a boastful rap villain song about how awesome he is, playing it in class
Explanation: He indulges his ego and gets amusement while wasting class time and annoying students.

Event: Chang performs nine-minute keytar solos regularly enough that Abed can time them
Explanation: His indulgence hurts his social image and patience of others while giving mild entertainment.

Event: Chang loudly bursts into scenes yelling “Did someone say CRAZY PERSON?!” when no one did
Explanation: He gains attention but strengthens perceptions of instability, while giving others some amusement and irritation.

Event: Professor Duncan delays an experiment to see how long subjects last and is pleased when Chang instantly explodes
Explanation: He gets fast, clear data with minimal extra effort and relatively minor harm to participants.

Event: Chang has an explosive tantrum on hearing the experiment is delayed, throwing furniture and screaming
Explanation: He vents frustration in a way that damages his reputation and property for no gain.

Event: Chang insults Steven Spielberg, ending a near-opportunity to become a movie star
Explanation: He sabotages his own major career chance while the rest of the world is largely unaffected or amused.

Event: Chang rejects the chance to be a better person as “Kevin” despite Abed’s encouragement in “Heroic Origins”
Explanation: He throws away a realistic path to reform and inclusion, to the detriment of himself and those around him.

Event: Chang comes out as “legit gay” in the finale after years of using “gay” as an insult
Explanation: He gains some self-acceptance and honesty and may encourage greater understanding, despite prior hypocrisy.
